1: David again assembled all the chosen men of Israel, thirty thousand. The assembly of 30,000 for the ark procession matches the size of the force sent to bring the ark from Shiloh before the Aphek disaster (1 Sam 4:10: Israel fell, 30,000 foot soldiers). The parallel is deliberate — the ark's history from Shiloh to Kiriath-jearim (where it has sat for 20 years since 1 Sam 7:1-2) to Jerusalem is being framed as a national event of the same magnitude as the Aphek crisis.
2: David arose and went with all the people who were with him from Baale-judah to bring up from there the ark of God, which is called by the name of YHWH of hosts who sits enthroned on the cherubim. Baale-judah (= Kiriath-jearim, Josh 15:9, 'the heights of Judah') was where the ark had rested in the house of Abinadab since the Philistines returned it (1 Sam 7:1). The full name of the ark — 'called by the name of YHWH of hosts who sits enthroned on the cherubim' — is its most theologically loaded designation: the ark is both the footstool and the throne-base of YHWH's invisible presence, flanked by the cherubim figures. The 'sitting on the cherubim' motif appears in the iconography of ANE royal thrones, where winged composite creatures form the throne base.
3: And they set the ark of God on a new cart and brought it out of the house of Abinadab on the hill. Uzzah and Ahio, the sons of Abinadab, were guiding the new cart. The 'new cart' method was the procedure the Philistines had improvised when returning the ark (1 Sam 6:7-8) — it was not the prescribed Israelite method. Numbers 4:15 required the sons of Kohath (a Levitical family) to carry the ark on poles inserted through rings, on their shoulders. Numbers 7:9 explicitly states: 'to the sons of Kohath he gave none, because they were charged with the service of the holy things that had to be carried on the shoulder.' The Philistine cart-method, adopted without correction, sets up the crisis at the threshing floor.
4: And they brought it out of the house of Abinadab, which was on the hill, with the ark of God. Ahio went before the ark. The procession is festive and apparently well-organized — Uzzah walking beside/behind the cart to steady it if needed, Ahio walking ahead. The disaster that follows is not from lack of reverence or festivity but from an incorrect method of transport that required human hands to correct what poles would have prevented.
5: And David and all the house of Israel were celebrating before YHWH with all kinds of instruments made of fir wood, with lyres and harps and tambourines and castanets and cymbals. The musical celebration accompanies the ark procession — the same combination of instruments appears in the later psalms associated with the ark (Ps 150). The fir wood (bərôšîm, possibly cypress) instruments represent the finest available materials. The festive procession will be interrupted and then resumed in ch6 after a three-month pause.
6: And when they came to the threshing floor of Nacon (gōren nāḵôn), Uzzah put out his hand to the ark of God and took hold of it, for the oxen stumbled. The threshing floor (gōren) was a flat outdoor surface where grain was winnowed — the location suggests the cart traveled on a road near a threshing floor when the oxen stumbled. The stumble of the oxen is the occasion for the crisis, but the 1 Chr 15:13 retrospective identifies the root cause: 'because you did not carry it at the first, YHWH our God broke out against us, because we did not seek him according to the rule.'
7: And the anger of YHWH was kindled against Uzzah, and God struck him down there because of his error (ʿal-haššal), and he died there beside the ark of God. The word šal (error, irreverence, rashness) is rare and debated — it may mean 'rashness' or 'irreverence' rather than deliberate sin. Uzzah's death is not presented as punishment for malice but as the consequence of unauthorized contact with the ark. Numbers 4:15 explicitly warned: 'they must not touch any sacred thing, or they will die.' The transport method had already created a situation where human hands would be needed to prevent the ark from falling — the death of Uzzah is the outworking of the incorrect method, not merely of the contact itself.
8: And David was angry because YHWH had burst forth against Uzzah. And that place is called Perez-uzzah to this day. David's anger (wayyiḥar lǝdāwid) mirrors the language of YHWH's anger in v7 — a momentary shock response to what appeared to be an unjust death. Perez-uzzah (the breaking-out against Uzzah) is a commemorative place-name for the site of the crisis. David's anger is not condemned by the narrator but resolved in fear (v9) and eventually in proper inquiry about the correct procedure (1 Chr 15:2,13).
9: And David was afraid of YHWH that day, and he said, How can the ark of YHWH come to me? The fear (wayyîrāʾ dāwid) replaces anger as David reconsiders the significance of the event. His question — 'how can the ark come to me?' — reflects the recognition that approaching the holy presence of YHWH requires knowledge and procedure that he has not yet sought. The ark procession is halted; David diverts the ark to the nearby house of Obed-edom rather than continuing to Jerusalem.
10: So David was not willing to take the ark of YHWH into the city of David. But David took it aside to the house of Obed-edom the Gittite. Obed-edom the Gittite is identified in 1 Chr 26:4-8 as a Levite, from Gath-rimmon (a Levitical city in Manasseh, Josh 21:24-25) rather than the Philistine Gath — the 'Gittite' designation reflects his city of origin, not his ethnicity. As a Levite, Obed-edom's household was an appropriate temporary location for the ark, which explains why it could stay there safely while it had killed Uzzah on the cart.
11: And the ark of YHWH remained in the house of Obed-edom the Gittite three months, and YHWH blessed Obed-edom and all his household. The three-month blessing of Obed-edom's house becomes the news that reaches David (v12) and motivates him to resume the procession. The blessing is the positive counterpart to Uzzah's death: the ark brings death to those who approach it incorrectly and blessing to those who house it with reverence. 1 Chr 15-16 supplies the narrative of how David arranged for the Levites to carry the ark properly for the resumed procession.
12: And it was told King David, YHWH has blessed the household of Obed-edom and all that belongs to him, because of the ark of God. So David went and brought up the ark of God from the house of Obed-edom to the city of David with rejoicing. The three-month interval allowed David to discover the correct procedure (1 Chr 15:2: 'No one but the Levites may carry the ark of God, for YHWH chose them to carry the ark and to minister to him forever'). The resumed procession comes with proper Levitical transport and the sacrifices described in vv13-14.
13: And when those who bore the ark of YHWH had gone six steps, he sacrificed an ox and a fattened animal. The sacrifice after six steps is the ritual acknowledgment that the resumed procession is operating under YHWH's blessing — the progression proceeds six steps (representing the six days of creation or simply a unit of distance) and then stops for sacrifice. The Levitical bearers are implied by the verb 'those who bore' — the cart has been replaced by human carriers on shoulders as required by the Torah.
14: And David danced before YHWH with all his might. And David was wearing a linen ephod (ʾēpôḏ bāḏ). The linen ephod was a priestly garment (1 Sam 2:18: Samuel wore one as a boy; 1 Sam 22:18: the priests of Nob wore them). David's wearing it suggests he assumed a quasi-priestly role in the procession — the Davidic king exercising a priestly function at the transfer of the ark. The later tradition develops David's priestly-royal role (Ps 110:4: 'You are a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek'). David's dancing before YHWH with all his might is the Hebrew expression of complete, uninhibited worship: məkarker bəkol-ʿōz (leaping/whirling with all his strength).
15: So David and all the house of Israel brought up the ark of YHWH with shouting and with the sound of the horn (bəqôl šôpār). The shofar (šôpār) was used at theophanic events (Exod 19:16,19: Sinai), battle signals (Num 10:9), and royal coronations (2 Sam 15:10; 1 Kgs 1:34). Its use here marks the ark's arrival in Jerusalem as a quasi-coronation event — YHWH the king returning to his city in triumph.
16: As the ark of YHWH came into the city of David, Michal the daughter of Saul looked out of the window and saw King David leaping and dancing before YHWH, and she despised him in her heart. Michal's watching from the window (wattaśqēp... bəʿaḏ haḥallôn) is an ironic echo of the 'watching from the window' motif in Israelite narrative: Sisera's mother watches from the window waiting for her son's return (Judg 5:28); Jezebel looks from a window at Jehu's approach (2 Kgs 9:30). In each case, the woman at the window represents a power about to be displaced. Michal's contempt for David's worship will result in her permanent childlessness — the house of Saul ends with her.
17: And they brought in the ark of YHWH and set it in its place, inside the tent that David had pitched for it. And David offered burnt offerings and peace offerings before YHWH. The temporary tent shelter (as opposed to a permanent temple) echoes the tabernacle tradition — YHWH has dwelt in a tent throughout the wilderness period and the judges period. David pitches a new tent for the ark in Jerusalem, the forerunner of the Temple. His personal offering of burnt offerings and peace offerings — normally a priestly function — reflects the Davidic priestly-royal role at the ark.
18: And when David had finished offering the burnt offerings and the peace offerings, he blessed the people in the name of YHWH of hosts. The royal blessing of the people after the sacrifice is another quasi-priestly act — the Aaronic blessing (Num 6:24-26) was the priests' function. David's blessing 'in the name of YHWH of hosts' connects to the ark's full title (v2) and to the military theology of the ark's presence.
19: And he distributed among all the people, the whole multitude of Israel, both men and women, to each a cake of bread, a portion of meat, and a cake of raisins. And all the people departed, each to his house. The distribution of food to all Israel — regardless of gender or social status — is a statement of covenant solidarity. The food gifts (bread cake, meat portion, raisin cake) are the same kinds of items given at feasts and sacred meals. The equal distribution signals that the ark's arrival is a benefit for all Israel, not just the cultic specialists.
20: David returned to bless his household. And Michal the daughter of Saul came out to meet David and said, How the king of Israel honored himself today, uncovering himself today before the eyes of his servants' female servants, as one of the vulgar fellows shamelessly uncovers himself! Michal's accusation — that David exposed himself inappropriately before the servant girls — is likely an exaggeration born of contempt rather than a literal report. The linen ephod was a garment that covered more than the usual soldier's battle gear but less than royal robes; dancing in it might have exposed him in ways a king's dignity was not supposed to permit. Michal's frame of reference is Saulide royal dignity; David's is uninhibited devotion to YHWH.
21: And David said to Michal, It was before YHWH, who chose me above your father and above all his house, to appoint me as prince over Israel, the people of YHWH — and I will celebrate before YHWH. David's response explicitly invokes the election theology: YHWH chose David over Saul. The contrast between Michal's framework (royal dignity) and David's (YHWH's choice) reveals the theological divide between the Saulide understanding of kingship (honor and dignity before the people) and the Davidic understanding (the king as YHWH's servant whose first obligation is worship, not self-presentation).
22: I will become even more undignified than this, and I will be abased in my own eyes. But by the female servants of whom you have spoken, by them I shall be held in honor. David's willingness to be further undignified before YHWH — and his prediction that the very women Michal mocked him for performing before will honor him — is the inversion of the honor calculus Michal operates with. The pattern is the same as the Magnificat's reversal: the proud are brought low and the humble are exalted.
23: And Michal the daughter of Saul had no child to the day of her death. The closing note of Michal's childlessness is the narrative consequence of her contempt for David's worship. The end of Michal's line is the end of Saul's direct line through David's house — the dynastic future belongs entirely to David's sons by other wives. The phrase 'daughter of Saul' (rather than 'wife of David') throughout this episode keeps her Saulide identity in the foreground.
